title: Laws of Robotics and Party Rights

jokes.text: Which always confuses me, because black means bad.
jokes.setup: Reference to putting the school 'in the black' financially
jokes.punchline: Character takes the expression literally about color meaning bad
jokes.explanation: Simple observational humor about literal interpretation of idiom. Functional setup for racial commentary that follows.

dialogue.speaker: Dean Pelton
dialogue.line: We need to get this school back in the black.

dialogue.speaker: Chang
dialogue.line: But Dean, I'm not a color. I'm a person.

jokes.text: When it's a cat or banana or a shirt that makes you think you've lost more weight than you have.
jokes.punchline: Increasingly specific examples ending with oddly personal weight loss delusion
jokes.explanation: Good escalation from general to weirdly specific personal detail. The weight loss shirt is an unexpectedly relatable absurdity.

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: Black licorice? Disgusting. Black olives? No thank you. Blackmail? A crime. See, black means bad.

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: Black death? Killed millions. Blackheart? A villain. Blacklist? You don't want to be on it.

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: Black lung disease? Terrible. Black market? Illegal. And black belt? Well, that's just what I need to lose ten pounds.

jokes.text: Nice save. Sorry about that, man.
jokes.punchline: Another character apologetically acknowledging the awkwardness
jokes.explanation: Meta acknowledgment of social awkwardness sets up the deeper commentary about racial dynamics.

jokes.text: I have a brain the size of Jupiter. I'm nobody's fourth Ghostbuster.
jokes.setup: Being asked to give racial 'all-clear' approval
jokes.punchline: Rejecting token minority role with grandiose self-description and pop culture reference
jokes.explanation: Excellent character-specific line that perfectly captures Elroy's intelligence and dignity. The Ghostbusters reference is both specific and universally understood as the 'unnecessary addition.'

dialogue.speaker: Elroy
dialogue.line: No. I'm not gonna be the guy who gives racial all-clear approval to everything you do. I'm a spaceman. I come from the future. I'm here to teach you about friendship and the cosmos.

jokes.text: In fact, before you got here, they had two.
jokes.setup: Claiming the group doesn't think in racial terms
jokes.punchline: Immediately contradicting by revealing they counted minority members
jokes.explanation: Sharp subversion of the previous claim about not thinking racially. The immediate contradiction is well-timed.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: We don't think in racial terms here. We're a group that transcends race.

dialogue.speaker: Troy
dialogue.line: Yeah, we're colorblind.

dialogue.speaker: Shirley
dialogue.line: I'm glad you said that, because I was worried about being the only black person here.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: You're not. We have two Hispanics, an Asian, and a lesbian.

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: I'm not a lesbian, Jeff.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Not yet.

jokes.text: They had to often step back and remind themselves that they weren't one collective ray of light.
jokes.punchline: Absurdly grandiose metaphor for white privilege awareness
jokes.explanation: Wonderfully pretentious and absurd metaphor that perfectly captures performative woke-ness. Very Community-style elevated absurdity.

jokes.text: Is this a cult? Are you gonna eat me?
jokes.setup: The bizarre 'collective ray of light' comment
jokes.punchline: Reasonable escalation to cult fears with cannibalism
jokes.explanation: Good escalation and character reaction. Elroy's practical response to the group's weirdness is in character.

dialogue.speaker: Elroy
dialogue.line: You know what? I'm starting to think this group might be a cult.

dialogue.speaker: Elroy
dialogue.line: First you're all standing around like a collective ray of light, and next thing I know, you're eating people.

jokes.text: Nice grammar.
jokes.setup: Britta's double negative 'not, not a rager'
jokes.punchline: Annie's sarcastic correction
jokes.explanation: Very Annie - correcting grammar in the middle of an argument. Good character consistency.

dialogue.speaker: Annie
dialogue.line: Wow, Britta. A double negative. So that means it IS a rager. Congratulations on accidentally telling the truth.

jokes.text: Okay, you have a rule against parties, which I'm sure is a world record for uptightness, so let's celebrate that.
jokes.punchline: Suggesting they celebrate being uptight, which would itself be a party
jokes.explanation: Good ironic escalation that captures Britta's rebellious nature and the logical paradox of celebrating uptightness.

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: We should celebrate being uptight. That would be a party!

jokes.text: You're a coucher.
jokes.setup: Britta not having a real bedroom
jokes.punchline: Making 'couch sleeper' sound like a derogatory category
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective wordplay that creates a new derogatory term. Very quotable and captures the power dynamic.

dialogue.speaker: Annie
dialogue.line: So you're basically a couch sleeper.

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: Why does that sound like I'm a subcategory of human?

dialogue.speaker: Annie
dialogue.line: Because you are.

jokes.text: She sleeps on the couch. She's a coucher.
jokes.punchline: Immediately explaining and repeating the insult
jokes.explanation: Reinforces the new term but explaining the joke weakens it. Still in character for Annie's precision.

dialogue.speaker: Annie
dialogue.line: A coucher! You know, someone who coughs and coughs but won't go to a doctor. You just keep coughing on everyone like some kind of coucher!

jokes.text: There's no government to overthrow here.
jokes.setup: Britta's rebellious attitude about the apartment rules
jokes.punchline: Reducing Britta's activism to pointless rebellion
jokes.explanation: Perfect character-based humor that captures both Annie's authority and Britta's reflexive activism. Well-constructed.

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: I'm not going to follow some arbitrary rules just because they're written down. Rules are meant to be questioned!

dialogue.speaker: Annie
dialogue.line: Britta, it's about keeping the apartment clean and respecting everyone's space.

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: That's exactly what they want you to think. This is about control, man. I'm fighting for our freedom.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: You're fighting to leave your dishes in the sink.

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: It's a metaphor!

dialogue.speaker: Annie
dialogue.line: It's really not.

jokes.text: Nice irony.
jokes.setup: Annie's 'You're overthrowing if I say so!' autoritarianism
jokes.punchline: Britta correctly identifying the ironic power dynamic
jokes.explanation: Good meta-commentary on the power dynamic, though relatively simple. Shows Britta's awareness.

dialogue.speaker: Annie
dialogue.line: You're overthrowing if I say so!

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: That's literally the definition of authoritarianism.

jokes.text: I resent the idea that this would alarm me.
jokes.setup: Frankie saying 'Don't be alarmed' about the robot
jokes.punchline: Offended preemptive defensiveness about being alarmed
jokes.explanation: Good character beat showing defensive pride about being tech-savvy or unflappable.

dialogue.speaker: Frankie Dart
dialogue.line: Don't be alarmed.

dialogue.speaker: Unknown character
dialogue.line: Why would I be alarmed?

dialogue.speaker: Frankie Dart
dialogue.line: Well, it's a robot.

dialogue.speaker: Unknown character
dialogue.line: So? I'm not some luddite who gets scared of robots.

jokes.text: Seamless, Frankie.
jokes.setup: Frankie's awkward pause mid-sentence on the robot
jokes.punchline: Sarcastic praise for obviously non-seamless technology
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective sarcasm about technology failing. Economical and well-timed.

dialogue.speaker: Frankie
dialogue.line: Yeah, that was... that was really seamless.

jokes.text: That's the black guy in every sci-fi movie. I'll allow it.
jokes.setup: Someone expressing fear about prisoners with robots
jokes.punchline: Self-aware acknowledgment of his role as the skeptical black character
jokes.explanation: Brilliant meta-commentary that acknowledges racial tropes in genre fiction while subverting them. Perfect for Elroy's character and very Community-style meta humor.

dialogue.speaker: Elroy
dialogue.line: I'm not comfortable with this. We're giving robots to prisoners? That's how you get a robot uprising. I've seen enough movies to know how this ends.

dialogue.speaker: Elroy
dialogue.line: And before you say anything, yes, I know I'm the skeptical black guy. That's my thing. But I'm right about this one.

jokes.text: They'll rape us. They'll rape us all.
jokes.punchline: Immediate jump to worst-case sexual assault scenario
jokes.explanation: Cringe humor based on irrational fear escalation. Somewhat cheap but effectively uncomfortable and sets up the correction.

dialogue.speaker: Dean Pelton
dialogue.line: We have prisoners on campus for the work-study program, and I'm concerned about safety.

dialogue.speaker: Chang
dialogue.line: You're right to be concerned. What if they sexually assault someone?

jokes.text: They can't rape you, because their genitals will be hundreds of miles away.
jokes.punchline: Literal technical explanation of why robot prisoners can't commit sexual assault
jokes.explanation: Excellent absurdist logic that's both practical and ridiculous. Perfect Frankie-style literal problem-solving that misses the emotional point.

dialogue.speaker: Frankie
dialogue.line: I'm worried about prisoner rape.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: Well, if they're robots, they don't have the biological capacity for sexual assault. Their programming is strictly custodial.

jokes.text: They're iPads on sticks.
jokes.punchline: Reducing scary prisoners to mundane consumer electronics
jokes.explanation: Perfect reductive description that deflates all the drama. Extremely economical and captures Frankie's practical mindset.

dialogue.speaker: Frankie
dialogue.line: We need to be concerned about dangerous convicts in our community.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: What are you talking about? They're not dangerous, they're just like... regular people.

dialogue.speaker: Frankie
dialogue.line: Jeff, these are convicted felons we're discussing.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Yeah, but if you think about it, they're basically just iPads with abandonment issues.

jokes.text: Hey guys, I'm moon walking.
jokes.punchline: Chang randomly announces he's moonwalking on his robot
jokes.explanation: Very Chang - completely ignoring the serious conversation to play with technology. Good character moment.

dialogue.speaker: Chang
dialogue.line: I'm moonwalking on my robot.

jokes.text: Oh, I walked into that one.
jokes.setup: Jeff saying 'Do it' after Dean says he'd do anything Jeff says
jokes.punchline: Dean acknowledging he set himself up
jokes.explanation: Good self-aware moment that acknowledges the setup. Very much in character for the Dean's relationship with Jeff.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Do it.

dialogue.speaker: Dean
dialogue.line: I walked right into that, didn't I?

jokes.text: Gross, who farted? Oh wait, nevermind.
jokes.punchline: Realizes he can't smell through the robot
jokes.explanation: Simple physical comedy about robot limitations. Fits Chang's character but not particularly clever.

dialogue.speaker: Chang
dialogue.line: Wait, I can see you, I can hear you, I can even feel the vibrations through my sensors, but... I can't smell anything.

dialogue.speaker: Chang
dialogue.line: What kind of half-baked telepresence technology doesn't include smell?

jokes.text: Then give me what the ceremony would cost.
jokes.setup: Professor Emeritus ceremony that doesn't pay more
jokes.punchline: Jeff wanting the ceremony budget as cash instead
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of Jeff's mercenary attitude. Practical logic that deflates ceremonial importance.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Wait, so this Professor Emeritus thing doesn't come with a raise?

dialogue.speaker: Dean Pelton
dialogue.line: It's an honor, Jeff. A ceremonial recognition of your years of service.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Right, but honor doesn't pay my mortgage. How much is the ceremony budget?

dialogue.speaker: Dean Pelton
dialogue.line: Approximately three thousand dollars.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Perfect. I'll take it in cash.

jokes.text: if I see any race gangs forming, I will have no choice but to adjust the contrast on your screens until you all look the same.
jokes.setup: Dean addressing potential prison gang problems
jokes.punchline: Technical solution to racial tension via screen settings
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist solution that perfectly captures the Dean's oblivious approach to serious issues. The technical literalism is perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Dean Pelton
dialogue.line: We need to address the racial tension in our prison program before it becomes a real problem.

dialogue.speaker: Dean Pelton
dialogue.line: I've implemented a simple solution: I've adjusted all the monitors in the prison to black and white.

dialogue.speaker: Dean Pelton
dialogue.line: If they can't see race, they can't be racist.

jokes.text: so be nice, to me.
jokes.punchline: Reveals it's specifically about protecting himself
jokes.explanation: Perfect misdirection that reveals the Dean's self-centered interpretation of policy. Very economical and in character.

dialogue.speaker: Dean Pelton
dialogue.line: At Greendale, we have a very strict anti-bullying policy. We believe that every student deserves to feel safe and respected on campus.

dialogue.speaker: Dean Pelton
dialogue.line: In fact, this policy is so important to me, I've made it my personal mission to ensure it's enforced... particularly when it comes to protecting myself from mean comments about my cardigan collection.

jokes.text: it's like watching magicians bully jugglers.
jokes.punchline: Comparing it to one marginally skilled group bullying another
jokes.explanation: Excellent absurdist comparison that perfectly captures the ridiculousness of the situation. Very quotable and original.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Wait, so we're picking on prisoners? That's like one marginally skilled group bullying another.

jokes.text: I was a phony lawyer, I got caught, and now I teach a pretty phony class.
jokes.setup: Willy asking if he can teach him to get out of prison
jokes.punchline: Jeff's honest self-assessment of his fraudulent credentials
jokes.explanation: Great character moment showing Jeff's rare honesty about his situation. The parallel structure works well.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Look, I appreciate the sentiment, but I have to be honest with you - my law degree is from an online university that no longer exists, I've never actually passed the bar, and I'm pretty sure half the stuff I've told people about my legal experience was made up on the spot.

jokes.text: But, you might learn a thing or two. And I mean literally a thing or two.
jokes.setup: Saying students might learn in his class
jokes.punchline: Clarifying he means exactly two things, not the idiom
jokes.explanation: Good literal interpretation joke that continues Jeff's honest self-assessment of his minimal teaching.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: You might learn one or two things in my class.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: And I mean exactly two things. Not the idiom. Literally two things. That's all you're getting.

jokes.text: Hey, and they call me the criminal.
jokes.setup: Jeff admitting to being a fraud
jokes.punchline: Pointing out the irony of who's labeled criminal
jokes.explanation: Good ironic observation that sets up the moral ambiguity between Jeff and Willy.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: I'm a fraud. I never actually went to high school.

dialogue.speaker: Willy
dialogue.line: At least you went to college.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: I didn't go to college either. I forged my transcripts to get into this school.

dialogue.speaker: Willy
dialogue.line: So you're telling me you're a criminal, and I'm the one they kicked out?

jokes.text: I know the main difference between us is an orange jumpsuit.
jokes.punchline: Reducing their differences to just clothing/circumstances
jokes.explanation: Good observational humor that establishes the parallel between characters while being economical.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: You know, Willy, when you really think about it, we're not that different. We both want respect, we both want to be seen as more than just our circumstances.

dialogue.speaker: Willy
dialogue.line: That's beautiful, man.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Right? I mean, strip away the designer clothes, the law degree, the chiseled jawline—

dialogue.speaker: Willy
dialogue.line: And you're basically me.

jokes.text: You have an iPad.
jokes.setup: Jeff saying they're similar except for jumpsuit
jokes.punchline: Willy pointing out another key difference
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective correction. Very economical and maintains the absurdist reality of the situation.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: We're basically the same, except for the jumpsuit.

dialogue.speaker: Willy
dialogue.line: And the fact that I'm a figment of your imagination.

jokes.text: Just don't hide it. You know, once you take another man's life, the least you can do is carry it with you.
jokes.setup: Admitting to murder but saying he's not proud
jokes.punchline: Twisted moral philosophy about owning your murders
jokes.explanation: Darkly comic philosophy that's both disturbing and oddly principled. Good character establishment for Willy.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: I killed a man.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: But I'm not proud of it.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: See, the thing is, if you're going to kill a man, you gotta own it. You can't go around feeling guilty about your murders. That's not taking responsibility, that's just being a coward.

jokes.text: Are you carrying any murders, Mr. Winger?
jokes.punchline: Directly asking Jeff about his murder count
jokes.explanation: Good follow-up that makes the question personal and uncomfortable. The phrasing is memorably creepy.

dialogue.speaker: Willy
dialogue.line: I have a philosophy about carrying murders. You only carry the murders you can handle.

dialogue.speaker: Willy
dialogue.line: Jeff, how many murders are you carrying?

jokes.text: I hear you have to have strong feelings for someone you kill. I try to reserve that stuff for myself.
jokes.punchline: Jeff's narcissistic take on murder requiring emotional investment
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark character comedy that perfectly captures Jeff's self-absorption even in discussing murder. Very quotable and cleverly constructed.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: You know what? I can't even commit murder. It requires too much emotional investment. I'd have to care about someone enough to want them dead, and frankly, most people aren't worth the effort.

jokes.text: Who wants to watch Planet Earth? Oh, look, ice worlds, oh the majesty.
jokes.punchline: Jeff's awkward transition to nature documentary with fake enthusiasm
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Jeff's discomfort and lazy teaching. The forced enthusiasm is appropriately awkward.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: So... that murder thing. Pretty crazy, right? I mean, obviously murder is bad. Very bad. The worst, really.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Anyway! Did you know that the African elephant is the largest land mammal? Fascinating creatures. They can communicate over distances of up to six miles using infrasound. Nature is truly amazing!

jokes.text: I'd like to make this moment worth popping back to from some point in the future.
jokes.setup: Abed explaining his old pop back gag habit
jokes.punchline: Meta-commentary on setting up callbacks
jokes.explanation: Perfect Abed meta-commentary on narrative structure. Very character-specific and shows his analytical approach to storytelling.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: I used to do this thing where I'd set up a joke in the pilot, then call it back later in the season. It was very meta.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: But then I realized I was spending so much time setting up callbacks that I wasn't actually making jokes in the present moment.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: So now I just make jokes. And if they happen to callback to something, great. If not, that's also great, because at least I'm not trapped in a narrative structure of my own design.

jokes.text: I'm glad I kicked that habit it was getting a little joyless.
jokes.setup: His explanation of pop back gags
jokes.punchline: Self-awareness that his meta-commentary was becoming mechanical
jokes.explanation: Good meta-commentary about the show's own tendency toward self-reference. Shows character growth for Abed.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: Pop back gags are a sitcom staple where a character reappears after being written out, usually with an explanation that subverts expectations. The meta-humor comes from acknowledging the trope itself.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: But I've been doing this so much that I've stopped actually being meta and just... became the thing I was making fun of. Now I'm just mechanically aware of tropes instead of genuinely commenting on them.

jokes.text: They're like conventions for every subject too boring to have their own convention.
jokes.setup: Explaining why he doesn't like parties
jokes.punchline: Defining parties as conventions for boring subjects
jokes.explanation: Brilliant observational humor that perfectly captures Abed's perspective. The comparison is both accurate and hilariously specific. Extremely quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: Parties are just conventions for boring subjects. Like how action movies have explosions, parties have small talk. They're both just filler to distract you from the lack of real content.

jokes.text: That was for my movie Reverse Godzilla. I also thought it might make a good pop back.
jokes.setup: Reference to filling apartment with lizards
jokes.punchline: Absurd movie concept plus meta-commentary callback
jokes.explanation: Good callback to the pop back discussion while introducing an absurd movie concept. Very Abed.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: We could fill the entire apartment with lizards. It would be like a low-budget Jurassic Park, except the dinosaurs are just regular lizards and instead of running from them, we're living with them.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: Actually, that's not a bad pitch. 'Lizard House.' Coming this summer to a streaming service that desperately needs content.

jokes.text: I make movies, Annie makes rules, you make parties. Her rules cancel out your parties, but I'm not involved, I just make movies.
jokes.punchline: Reducing everyone to simple functions with mathematical logic
jokes.explanation: Excellent Abed analysis that reduces complex social dynamics to simple equations. Perfect character voice and logic.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: Everyone in this apartment can be reduced to simple mathematical functions. Troy is a linear function—predictable, consistent. Shirley's a polynomial—complex, with multiple peaks and valleys. Annie's exponential—she grows exponentially with stress. Jeff is a constant—he never changes, he just stays cool. Britta's a logarithm—diminishing returns on every decision she makes. And I'm the function that describes all the other functions.

jokes.text: What are categories in Prison Jeopardy?
jokes.punchline: Treating the slang as game show categories
jokes.explanation: Good escalation that transforms intimidating prison talk into game show format. Shows Jeff deflecting tension with humor.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Willy, you can't just use prison slang like that in everyday conversation.

dialogue.speaker: Willy
dialogue.line: What do you mean? 'Shank,' 'Lockdown,' 'Yard Time' — these are perfectly good words.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Those are prison terms, Willy. You sound like you're hosting a game show.

dialogue.speaker: Willy
dialogue.line: For $200: A stabbing implement made from a toothbrush. What is a shank?

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: See? That's exactly what I'm talking about.

jokes.text: Did you just try to murder me?
jokes.setup: Willy trying to push him down stairs
jokes.punchline: Jeff's incredulous reaction to attempted murder
jokes.explanation: Good reaction beat but relatively straightforward. Sets up the escalating paranoia sequence.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Whoa, whoa, whoa! Did you just try to push me down the stairs?

jokes.text: You're trying as hard as you can right now to kill me.
jokes.punchline: Escalating to maximum effort murder attempt
jokes.explanation: Good escalation that gets more absurd. The 'as hard as you can' adds a specific absurdist touch.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: I didn't attempt to murder anyone!

dialogue.speaker: Dean Pelton
dialogue.line: That's what someone who attempted murder would say.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Okay, well I'm escalating this to a maximum effort murder attempt.

jokes.text: Oy vey.
jokes.punchline: Jeff's exasperated Yiddish expression
jokes.explanation: Simple reaction beat that shows Jeff's exasperation. Very economical but not particularly clever.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Oy vey!

jokes.text: Britta's Party, based on a true story of fun and no rules.
jokes.punchline: Treating a basic party as serious documentary subject
jokes.explanation: Good Abed meta-humor that elevates mundane events to film importance. The 'based on true story' adds to the pretension.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: I'm making a documentary about a party. We're treating it like it's a serious historical event. There's a steadicam following everyone, we have interviews with guests about their motivations, we're analyzing the snack choices as cultural indicators.

jokes.text: Everyone has a great time and parties like there's no tomorrow, then they leave. Sophia cleans up. The end.
jokes.punchline: Reducing it to the most basic possible plot
jokes.explanation: Good deflation of movie pretension with mundane reality. The cleaning detail is a nice practical touch.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: Boy meets girl. Boy loses girl. Boy gets girl back. Girl leaves boy for a astronaut.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: No wait, scratch that last part. Boy meets girl. Boy loses girl. Boy gets girl back. The end.

jokes.text: I see the real time stakes of Can't Hardly Wait, the subversive raunch of Risky Business, and the mumbly pointlessness of everything after 2003.
jokes.punchline: Ending with critique of modern mumblecore films
jokes.explanation: Excellent film criticism disguised as movie pitch. The escalation from specific films to general critique of an era is perfect Abed.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: My film draws from Godard, Truffaut, and early Soderbergh. It's a meditation on alienation in the digital age, shot entirely in natural light with a handheld camera aesthetic.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: But honestly, these modern mumblecore films are just bad acting hidden behind a veil of authenticity. They call it 'naturalistic' when really the actors just forgot their lines.

jokes.text: I moved in third. I'm a coucher.
jokes.setup: Her lack of authority in apartment decisions
jokes.punchline: Self-deprecatingly using Annie's insult
jokes.explanation: Good callback that shows Britta internalizing the insult. Character-appropriate self-defeat.

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: Fine, I guess I don't get a say in this apartment anyway. I'm just the permissive pothead with no backbone.

jokes.text: She's a coucher! I sleep on a couch!
jokes.punchline: Both characters emphasizing the point
jokes.explanation: Running gag continuation but becoming repetitive. The emphasis adds some energy but doesn't elevate it much.

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: Fine! I'm a coucher. I couch.

dialogue.speaker: Annie
dialogue.line: You couch so hard.

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: I couch so hard.

jokes.text: Before this is over, you'll beg for my forgiveness.
jokes.punchline: Ominous threat from normally sweet Annie
jokes.explanation: Good character subversion showing Annie's competitive side. The dramatic language is funny coming from her.

dialogue.speaker: Annie
dialogue.line: You know what? I'm done. I'm done being the nice one. I'm done compromising. You want to see what happens when Annie Edison stops being nice? Because I can make things very difficult for all of you. Very difficult.

jokes.text: Did he steal a driver's ed simulator? Did he start an illegal game of solitaire on his face?
jokes.punchline: Increasingly absurd iPad-based crimes
jokes.explanation: Excellent absurdist escalation that plays with the limitations of tablet-based crime. The solitaire on face is particularly creative.

dialogue.speaker: Frankie
dialogue.line: What else did Willy do?

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: He used an iPad to hack into the Pentagon.

dialogue.speaker: Frankie
dialogue.line: Okay, that's bad.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: Then he used an iPad to commit identity theft.

dialogue.speaker: Frankie
dialogue.line: That's worse.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: Then he used an iPad to teach dolphins how to use iPads.

jokes.text: Now where I'm from, you accuse somebody of something, you better have evidence, or he better be a minority.
jokes.setup: Jeff accusing him of attempted murder
jokes.punchline: Casual racism disguised as folksy wisdom
jokes.explanation: Dark humor that reveals Willy's casual bigotry through seemingly reasonable legal philosophy. Well-constructed character revelation.

dialogue.speaker: Willy
dialogue.line: Well now, I didn't mean nothin' by it. Back where I come from, we just say things the way they are. If a fella's got a little extra melanin, that's just the good Lord's way of—

jokes.text: Now, I'm not worth murdering?
jokes.setup: Question about why a murderer would kill a teacher
jokes.punchline: Jeff taking offense at his low murder-worthiness
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jeff narcissism - being insulted that he's not worth murdering. Excellent misdirection from the real issue to ego.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Wait, why would a murderer kill a teacher? What, I'm not murder-worthy enough?

jokes.text: You're a rock n tour. You charm people with your slick and fancy words.
jokes.punchline: 'Rock n tour' mispronunciation of 'raconteur'
jokes.explanation: Good malapropism that shows Willy's limited vocabulary while trying to sound sophisticated. The music reference makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: Jeff is such a raconteur.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Actually, it's pronounced 'rock n tour.'

jokes.text: I don't cotton much to fancy words and book study. It's not how I got to where I am.
jokes.setup: Willy asking about technical legal definitions
jokes.punchline: Jeff ironically downplaying education despite being a lawyer-teacher
jokes.explanation: Good ironic character moment showing Jeff adopting folksy persona to compete with Willy. The self-deprecation is strategic.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: I mean, who needs to know the technical legal definition of anything? I'm a lawyer and I teach at a community college, so clearly education isn't that important.

jokes.text: Mr. Winger was born in dirt, Willy. Same as you I'm sure.
jokes.punchline: Claiming humble dirt origins
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of Jeff's fake persona. The dramatic language is appropriately overwrought for the performance.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: I'm just a simple man from humble dirt origins. My father was dirt, my mother was dirt. I was born in a dirt hospital, delivered by a dirt doctor.

jokes.text: Mr. Winger, when did you stop being funny?
jokes.punchline: Student breaking fourth wall about character change
jokes.explanation: Excellent meta-commentary that acknowledges Jeff's character shift. Very Community-style self-awareness delivered by perfect character choice.

dialogue.speaker: Garrett
dialogue.line: Did anyone else notice Jeff suddenly became competent at something?

jokes.text: Shouldn't have worn that petard if you didn't want to be hoisted by it.
jokes.setup: Jeff complaining about the program he supported
jokes.punchline: Misunderstanding 'hoist by your own petard' idiom
jokes.explanation: Good setup for the following explanation. Shows Frankie's literal mindedness while misusing an idiom.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: I can't believe this. I fought so hard to get this program approved, and now it's completely backfiring on me.

dialogue.speaker: Frankie
dialogue.line: Well, you know what they say — you're being hoist by your own petard.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: What? I'm being hoisted by my own... petard? What is a petard?

dialogue.speaker: Frankie
dialogue.line: It's an explosive device from medieval times. The phrase means you're suffering from your own plan.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: So you're saying I'm literally being blown up by a medieval bomb that I created?

dialogue.speaker: Frankie
dialogue.line: No, it's just a metaphor.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Because I'm pretty sure I would remember building a bomb.

jokes.text: I guess I just assume that in the old days a petard was a special outfit like a leotard with a lot of fancy buckles and loops on it. And that rich people would wear them when they were feeling especially smug. But then poor people could tie a rope to one of the loops and hoist them up a pole and then let them dangle there as punishment for being cocky.
jokes.punchline: Elaborate invented etymology for the phrase
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist invention that creates an entire historical scenario. The detail and internal logic make it hilarious and very quotable. Perfect Frankie character moment.

dialogue.speaker: Frankie
dialogue.line: Wait, what do you mean 'hoist by your own petard'? That's not a real thing.

dialogue.speaker: Frankie
dialogue.line: A petard is obviously a medieval French nobleman who specialized in self-sabotage. So if you were 'hoisted by your own petard,' it meant that Petard showed up at your castle and made you look foolish through his elaborate pranks.

dialogue.speaker: Frankie
dialogue.line: The phrase fell out of use after Petard retired to the countryside and took up cheese-making instead.

jokes.text: Never look it up. Your explanation is way better.
jokes.punchline: Jeff preferring the wrong but entertaining version
jokes.explanation: Perfect reaction that validates Frankie's creativity over accuracy. Good character moment showing Jeff appreciating the performance.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Wait, so you're telling me that's not what a petard is? Because I've been picturing this whole thing completely differently, and honestly, your version is way less fun.

jokes.text: Party like there's no tomorrow. What? There's no tomorrow. Party like it.
jokes.punchline: Frustration with actors not understanding the concept
jokes.explanation: Good Abed frustration with literal interpretation of metaphorical direction. Shows his analytical approach to filmmaking.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: No, no, no! You're not getting it. This is a party scene. People at parties don't just stand there reciting lines like robots.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: You need to interact, to be present, to actually BE at a party. It's method acting, people!

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: Why is this so hard? I'm literally just asking you to do what you do every weekend.

jokes.text: I know most of you are like thirty.
jokes.setup: Addressing party guests about not busting them
jokes.punchline: Acknowledging the age inappropriateness of party concerns
jokes.explanation: Good observational humor about community college demographics. Shows Dean's awareness of the absurdity.

dialogue.speaker: Dean Pelton
dialogue.line: I'm not going to bust you for having a party. I'm way too cool for that.

dialogue.speaker: Dean Pelton
dialogue.line: But I am going to ask you to keep it down because I have a very important breakfast meeting tomorrow morning, and I need my beauty sleep. Which, let's be honest, I need a lot of.

jokes.text: Did someone order a prison break!
jokes.punchline: Treating scary concept as party entertainment
jokes.explanation: Good juxtaposition of threatening concept with party atmosphere. Very Dean-like oblivious enthusiasm.

dialogue.speaker: Dean Pelton
dialogue.line: Everyone, I'd like you to meet our new party entertainment—prisoner robots!

dialogue.speaker: Dean Pelton
dialogue.line: They're programmed to simulate maximum security prison conditions, but don't worry—it's all in good fun!

dialogue.speaker: Dean Pelton
dialogue.line: Who wants to experience solitary confinement? It's like a spa day, but with more psychological trauma!

jokes.text: Try dancing harder. Is that as hard as you can dance?
jokes.punchline: Treating dancing as measurable effort
jokes.explanation: Good Abed direction that applies technical filmmaking to impossible task. The effort measurement is absurdly specific.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: Okay, everyone, I need you to treat dancing as measurable effort. I want to see sweat. I want to see commitment. This isn't just moving your body to music—this is a cinematic experience.

jokes.text: They keep dancing like it's today.
jokes.punchline: Criticizing dancing for being temporally accurate
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist criticism that perfectly captures Abed's impossible artistic demands. The temporal dancing critique is wonderfully specific and quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: Why are you all dancing like it's 1987? This party is supposed to be happening now.

jokes.text: Look at me. I'm a murderer. Can I be as cool as you now?
jokes.setup: Confronting Willy by holding him up
jokes.punchline: Jeff's sarcastic attempt to gain murderer credibility
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing Jeff's jealousy of Willy's criminal credibility. The desperation for coolness is very Jeff.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: You think I'm not a real killer? I've killed plenty of people. I killed a guy in a parking lot once. Well, I didn't kill him, but I definitely contributed to his death.

jokes.text: You are going to a better place with a stronger signal.
jokes.punchline: Technology pun on afterlife concept
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark humor that perfectly adapts traditional death euphemism to technology context. Very clever and economical.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Willy, Willy, can you hear me? Willy?

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: I think you're losing him.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: No, no, no. Willy, stay with me. Don't go into the light!

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: Jeff, I think the WiFi just went out.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Oh. Well, that explains the afterlife.

jokes.text: I can't look it up. You destroyed my device, and my heart.
jokes.setup: Jeff telling him to look up dignity
jokes.punchline: Can't research because of destroyed tablet plus emotional damage
jokes.explanation: Good escalation from practical problem to emotional damage. The parallel structure works well.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Why don't you look up dignity?

dialogue.speaker: Willy
dialogue.line: I can't. My tablet's destroyed and I'm emotionally damaged.

jokes.text: Wait. Where's your poop go? A tank. Gross! Get a house.
jokes.punchline: Blunt questions about waste management
jokes.explanation: Simple bathroom humor that works through bluntness and social awkwardness. Gets reaction but not particularly clever.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: So you're living in an RV? Where do you... you know... go to the bathroom?

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: There's a toilet in the back.

dialogue.speaker: Troy
dialogue.line: But where does it go?

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: Into a tank underneath. You have to empty it manually.

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: I have questions.

jokes.text: Ben, aren't you homeless? Yeah, but I poop in a house. Whose? Nice try.
jokes.punchline: Homeless person claiming superior bathroom arrangements
jokes.explanation: Good escalation that reveals absurd hierarchy among housing-insecure people. The 'nice try' response adds mystery.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Living in an RV? That's basically being homeless.

dialogue.speaker: Homeless Person
dialogue.line: Yeah, well at least I don't have to worry about my septic tank backing up into my shower.

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: He's got a point.

jokes.text: Don't question rules. Rules are good. I'm bad. All hail Annie.
jokes.punchline: Sarcastic complete surrender to Annie's authority
jokes.explanation: Character-appropriate sarcastic surrender

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: You know what? You're right, Annie. I'm sorry. From now on, I'm going to follow every single rule you make. No questions asked. I'll be the most obedient person in this study group.

dialogue.speaker: Annie
dialogue.line: Really?

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: Yes. In fact, I'm getting you a little bell so you can summon me whenever you need something. And I'll curtsy.

jokes.text: I'll need a broom, a remote control car, an iPad and an elaborate gyroscopically stabilized servomotor.
jokes.setup: Jeff needing to get on campus
jokes.punchline: Increasingly complex DIY robot requirements
jokes.explanation: Good escalation from simple to technical. Very Abed to have specific engineering solutions. The servomotor detail is perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: I need to get on campus.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: You're going to need a robot.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: What? Why would I need a robot?

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: Well, first you need wheels, then a motor, then a gyroscope for balance, then a solar panel for power, then a small nuclear reactor as a backup, then a quantum processor, then—

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Okay, stop. I'm just going to walk.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: Your funeral.

jokes.text: Parties are just booze, low lights, and loud music, so people can feel more, see less, and not have to listen to each other or themselves.
jokes.setup: Her realization about parties being stupid
jokes.punchline: Cynical but accurate breakdown of party mechanics
jokes.explanation: Excellent cynical observation that's both funny and insightful. The parallel structure and escalation work perfectly. Very quotable philosophy.

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: Parties are just an excuse for people to stand around and pretend they're having fun while actually just checking their phones and wondering when they can leave without seeming rude.

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: It's a collective agreement to waste time together. We're all just hostages in a social contract we never agreed to.

jokes.text: You're going to be punished in ways you won't understand for longer than you think is rational or possible.
jokes.setup: Chang asking what will happen to him
jokes.punchline: Ominous prediction about incomprehensible punishment
jokes.explanation: Dark and ominous response that perfectly captures the relationship dynamic. The specificity makes it both funny and threatening.

dialogue.speaker: Chang
dialogue.line: What will happen to me?

dialogue.speaker: Elroy
dialogue.line: Something worse than death. Something so terrible, so incomprehensible, that your mind will reject it as impossible.

jokes.text: the 13th amendment abolished slavery for everyone but convicts, and that we have no choice but to pay you $0.13 per hour or less.
jokes.punchline: Casual reference to constitutional loophole allowing prison slavery
jokes.explanation: Dark humor that exposes real legal truth about prison labor. The matter-of-fact delivery in wedding ceremony format is effective.

dialogue.speaker: Warden
dialogue.line: I now pronounce you husband and wife. And by the power vested in me by the state, I also pronounce you legally bound to perform labor without compensation, as permitted by the 13th Amendment.

jokes.text: And I try to steal as little as possible, just the basics. Pens, coffee filters, toilet paper, and one time, an overhead projector and a roll of carpet.
jokes.punchline: Escalation from small items to major equipment and materials
jokes.explanation: Good escalation from reasonable office supplies to absurdly large items. The specific details make it funnier and more believable.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Okay, fine. I've stolen things. Small things. A pen from a hotel, some towels.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: But then it got worse. I took a road sign. Then copper wiring from a construction site.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Last week I stole an entire air conditioning unit from the library loading dock.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: I don't even need an air conditioning unit. I just... I couldn't stop myself.

jokes.text: I'm a gorgeous face that works for you and considers you a friend.
jokes.punchline: Jeff's narcissistic but genuine declaration
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jeff moment that combines vanity with genuine affection. The escalation from 'pretty face' to 'gorgeous face' is perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: I'm not like Willy. I manipulate people, sure, but I do it for me. I'm honest about my selfishness. That's the difference.

jokes.text: Oh, how the mighty have fallen. Son of a bitch!
jokes.setup: Learning Willy isn't actually a murderer
jokes.punchline: Jeff's immediate shift from superiority to anger
jokes.explanation: Good character reaction showing Jeff's petty frustration at losing his moral high ground. The quick emotional shift is very Jeff.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Wait, so Willy's not actually a murderer? I've been feeling superior to him this whole time!

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: That is so annoying!

jokes.text: There's a kid outside throwing tennis balls against my RV.
jokes.punchline: Random specific complaint about RV harassment
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist detail that adds to Elroy's RV living situation. The specificity of tennis balls makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Elroy
dialogue.line: I don't see why we need to have a meeting about this. We're not robots.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: Actually, I've been thinking about robot meetings a lot lately.

dialogue.speaker: Elroy
dialogue.line: Well, I've been thinking about RV harassment, and nobody wants to talk about that.

jokes.text: There's a janitor on it right now.
jokes.setup: Chang claiming his robot is broken
jokes.punchline: Revealing he's actually just sharing it with janitor
jokes.explanation: Good visual gag setup that explains Chang's 'malfunction.' The mundane reality deflates the technical excuse.

dialogue.speaker: Chang
dialogue.line: My robot is broken!

dialogue.speaker: Frankie
dialogue.line: What happened to it?

dialogue.speaker: Chang
dialogue.line: Well, actually... I'm just sharing it with the janitor.

jokes.text: I think Britta's being arrested.
jokes.punchline: Misinterpreting control struggle as arrest
jokes.explanation: Good visual gag that interprets technical difficulties as law enforcement action. Fits Britta's character.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: What is happening here?

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: My robot is attacking me!

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: That's not an attack. It's a malfunction in the programming.

dialogue.speaker: Troy
dialogue.line: Call the police!

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Troy, it's not assaulting her. Look—it's just malfunctioning.

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: Yes! Thank you, Jeff!

jokes.text: Initiating override protocol. All in favor of this purchase? Affirmative. Very well, Abeds commence with world domination. Eradicate, eradicate, eradicate.
jokes.punchline: Immediate transformation into robot uprising
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation that transforms boring meeting into sci-fi threat. Perfect Abed move to control the technology narrative ending.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: I vote yes on the robot purchase.

dialogue.speaker: Robot
dialogue.line: Thank you for your vote. I am now in control.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: Oh no.

tmdb_overview: When prisoners roll into Greendale via telerobot, Jeff meets his match in a clever convict. Britta's plans for a raging house party backfire.
insight: Season 6's weakest episode: 82 jokes at 2.94/min yield just 68.4 impact.
